participants.reasoning: The extracted description refers to a much larger sample of vulnerable households and school-aged children from a community-randomized trial, whereas the official participants are 2008 children aged 0–5 in specific poor households with different eligibility criteria and age range.
participants.extracted: The study included vulnerable households in Manicaland Province, Zimbabwe, identified through a community-randomized trial conducted in 2009–2010. Households were eligible if they contained children under 18 years, were not in the wealthiest 20% of households, and met at least one vulnerability criterion (poorest quintile, one or more orphans, household head under 18, at least one chronically ill member, or at least one disabled member). For the current analysis, the sample was restricted to 1,697 households caring for 5,331 school-aged children ages 6–17 years at follow-up, drawn from an initial eligible sample of 8,797 children in 2,078 households. The final analytic sample came from three study arms: control, unconditional cash transfer (UCT), and conditional cash transfer (CCT).
participants.official: 2008 children (aged 0 to 5 years) in poor households that included one or more non‐welfare (other cash transfers for orphans or children) children (aged 0 to 17 years at baseline) and were headed by a child (aged 0 to 17 years) or cared for one or more orphan children (aged 0 to 17 years), disabled persons or chromically ill persons interviewed twice (baseline: July 2009 to September 2009; follow‐up: March 2011 to May 2011), 10 sites, Manicaland, Zimbabwe

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention details on the two cash-transfer arms, the payment amounts and schedule, and the CCT conditions relevant to the school-aged children analyzed.
interventions.extracted: The study evaluated two household-level cash transfer interventions implemented in rural Zimbabwe: an unconditional cash transfer (UCT) and a conditional cash transfer (CCT). Eligible vulnerable households received US$18 plus US$4 per child, up to three children, paid every two months to the household head. The UCT arm received the cash without requirements, while the CCT arm received the same cash transfer but had to meet conditions including applying for birth certificates for children, ensuring school attendance for children ages 6–17 of at least 90% per month, and having a household representative attend parenting skills classes; additional health-related conditions applied to younger children in the broader programme but were not relevant to the school-aged sample analyzed here.
interventions.official:
2 intervention groups and 1 control group
UCT: 10 sites, 763 children (included in this review as the intervention group)
CCT: 10 sites, 637 children (included in this review as CCT comparison group)
No cash transfer (pure control group): 10 sites, 608 children (included in this review as control group)

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include child chronic illness and disability and define school attendance differently, while the official outcomes include vaccination status and current school attendance, so the sets do not match.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure child health vulnerability and social protection outcomes at baseline and approximately 1.5 years follow-up (pre/post). Planned outcomes included child chronic illness, measured as a dichotomous yes/no indicator of being very sick for at least 3 months during the past 12 months; child disability, measured as a dichotomous yes/no indicator of any impairment seriously affecting routine work, school, or other daily functioning; birth registration status, measured as a dichotomous yes/no indicator of whether the child had obtained birth registration; and school attendance, measured as a dichotomous yes/no indicator of whether the child attended school at least 90% of days in the past month. These outcomes were analyzed for school-aged children aged 6–17 years in the follow-up sample.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcomes
Registered birth (dichotomous measure: had birth registered)
Up to date on vaccination calendar (dichotomous measure: was fully vaccinated)
Secondary outcomes
School attendance (dichotomous measure: attends school, currently)
